---
title: "4.9 Indexed, While, and NIO Traversals"
subtitle: "ArrayList Traversals"
format:
  clean-revealjs:
    slide-number: true
    css: unit4-shared.css
lang: zh-CN
---

## Course Directory

### Return to the course outline

[← Back to AP CSA / 返回课程目录](../../index.html)

## Indexed For Loops

### Use get and size

You can use a regular indexed `for` loop to process list elements.

`ArrayList` indices start at `0` just like array indices.

Instead of `[]`, use `get(index)` to get the value and `set(index, value)` to set the element.

If the index is outside `0` to `size() - 1`, the code throws an `IndexOutOfBoundsException`.

## Coding Exercise

### `activecode:: listForLoop`

The following code throws an `IndexOutOfBoundsException`.

Can you fix it?

```java
import java.util.*;

public class TestForLoop
{
    public static void main(String[] args)
    {
        ArrayList<Integer> myList = new ArrayList<Integer>();
        myList.add(50);
        myList.add(30);
        myList.add(20);
        int total = 0;
        for (int i = 0; i <= myList.size(); i++)
        {
            total = total + myList.get(i);
        }
        System.out.println(total);
    }
}
```

## listForLoop Test Targets

### Runestone checks

Expected output:

```text
100
```

The fixed loop should contain:

```java
i < myList.size()
```

## While Loop

### Object-oriented list worker

The example below demonstrates a `while` loop and an object-oriented approach.

The list is a field of the current object.

An instance method, rather than a class/static method, loops through the list.

## Coding Exercise

### `activecode:: listForEachLoopObj`

The following code removes a name from a list.

Set the `found` variable to the appropriate `true` or `false` values at line 13 and line 20.

## ListWorker Code with Blanks

::: {.code-scroll}
```java
import java.util.*;
public class ListWorker
{
   private ArrayList<String> nameList;

   public ListWorker(ArrayList<String> nameList)
   {
       this.nameList = nameList;
   }

   public boolean removeName(String name)
   {
       boolean found =   // true or false?
       int index = 0;
       while (index < nameList.size())
       {
           if (name.equals(nameList.get(index)))
           {
               nameList.remove(index);
               found =    // true or false?
           }
           else
           {
               index++;
           }
       }
       return found;
    }
```
:::

## ListWorker Main

```java
    public static void main(String[] args)
    {
        ArrayList<String> myList = new ArrayList<String>();
        myList.add("Amun");
        myList.add("Ethan");
        myList.add("Donnie");
        myList.add("Ethan");
        ListWorker listWorker = new ListWorker(myList);
        System.out.println(listWorker.nameList);
        listWorker.removeName("Ethan");
        System.out.println("After removing Ethan: "
                  + listWorker.nameList);
    }
}
```

Expected output:

```text
[Amun, Ethan, Donnie, Ethan]
After removing Ethan: [Amun, Donnie]
```

## Removing While Traversing

### Only increment when not removed

Be careful when you remove items from a list as you loop through it.

The method above only increments the index if an item was not removed from the list.

Removing an item shifts the remaining items to the left.

If you increment the index in all cases, you skip the element immediately after each removed element.

## Quick Check

### `mchoice:: qloopList_1`

For `[0, 0, 4, 2, 5, 0, 3, 0]`, what will `nums` contain after `numQuest`?

```java
int k = 0;
Integer zero = new Integer(0);
while (k < nums.size())
{
    if (nums.get(k).equals(zero))
        nums.remove(k);
    k++;
}
```

Correct answer: `[0, 4, 2, 5, 3]`.

Incrementing the index each time misses when there are two zeros in a row.

## Mixed-Up Code

### `parsonsprob:: listInsertParsons`

The method `insertInOrder` should add the passed name in alphabetic order to a private list field called `nameList`.

It includes one extra block that is not needed.

```java
// Block A
public void insertInOrder(String name)
{

// Block B
int index = 0;

// Block C
while (index < nameList.size() &&
       nameList.get(index).compareTo(name) < 0)
{

// Block D
index++;

// Block E
} // end while

// Block F
nameList.add(index, name);

// Block G
} // end method

// Distractor
nameList.add(name);
```

## Correct Order

### Insert in alphabetic order

```java
public void insertInOrder(String name)
{
    int index = 0;
    while (index < nameList.size() &&
           nameList.get(index).compareTo(name) < 0)
    {
        index++;
    } // end while
    nameList.add(index, name);
} // end method
```

## Reading in Files with java.nio.file

### Not covered in the AP CSA exam

The `java.nio.file` package provides an easier way to read files.

The `Files` class has a method called `readAllLines`.

It reads all lines in a file and returns them as a `List` of `String` objects.

`readAllLines` throws an `IOException` if the file cannot be read.

## readAllLines Pattern

```java
import java.nio.file.*;

// This connects and reads in the file in 1 line of code!
// It needs to be in a method that throws or handles IOException
List<String> lines = Files.readAllLines(Paths.get("data.txt"));
```

The advantage of a dynamic data structure is that you do not need to know how many lines you are going to store when you create it.

## Coding Exercise

### `activecode:: read-pokemon-file-nio`

Complete the code in the `main` method to read all lines of the file using `Files.readAllLines` into a `List<String>` named `lines`.

Add a loop that prints out the first 10 pokemon.

```java
import java.io.*;
import java.nio.file.*;
import java.util.*;

public class ReadData
{
    public static void main(String[] args) throws IOException
    {
        List<String> lines = Files.readAllLines(Paths.get("pokemon.csv"));
        // Add a loop that prints out the first 10 elements of the List lines
        // You can use the get method with Lists just like ArrayLists

    }
}
```

## read-pokemon-file-nio Test Targets

### Runestone checks

The output should contain at least 10 lines.

The code should contain a call to `get` with `lines`:

```java
lines.get
```

## Classroom Check

### A complete answer should include

::: {.tight-list}
- use `i < list.size()` rather than `i <= list.size()`
- use `get(i)` instead of bracket indexing
- keep the index unchanged after removing an item
- explain why adjacent matching removals can be skipped
- insert in sorted order by scanning until `compareTo(name) < 0` is false
- use `Files.readAllLines` only as a non-AP convenience note
:::

## End

### 4.9 Part 2 complete

Next: Student objects and Word Pairs.
